speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: When he come here, a fellow by the name of Nastrum, there in Minnesota, he knew him. And he wrote to this old couple, and he got through, finished up his military training there in Sweden then. And then he wrote to them, and then they told him about the United States. So he went to Minnesota today.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And then he started working on the railroad. That's why he was in Sweden. He was working in Timbren. He's a great healer with a broad accent, so then he got a job that he would come to the British on the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Northern Pacific?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Mm -hmm. Yeah, he went to Northern Pacific, Roxanne, Minnesota, through to Moscow. You see there, Wozler, in one way is to go to the Canadian Pacific. That's Northern Pacific by the way it goes into Canada. He called it Canadian Pacific in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he wound up coming out to Moscow because he was working on the railroad?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah. Well, before they got there, it was way up north there because they'd walked. He had his long cabin here, and they spent a winter with the Western working at home. Then they had to walk all the way from Plummer. That's where they were to catch the train to Plummer. Take their pack on their back and wear Plummer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he wound up coming to the dry ridge country?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To sell?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, I think one reason was, Henry and August Anderson, they were in here. And he got acquainted with them, I believe, back east.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: See, Henry Anderson, he homes in this hillier place up here. And August Anderson, he homes dead on a creek bottom down there below, but there's now Dan McKinsie's place.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: So, Antlina's Anderson, he, that's the place that Ted Kratzka got. That was the latest. There were three brothers here at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, he probably just came out here and found this land open, and had it been surveyed by then?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah. They surveyed it. Hammond used to live on the West Side, I had a crot of quick, you know, coming down from Little Burries, you know, when you come and see that little... place on the flat down there. Oh, that used to be the old Hammond place. And he was a surveyor. Him and Dad surveyed through this country.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And Samberg up here, Dan Tolson, he homesed the place across the road, 160 East and West. And Samberg and Alfred Johnson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's the one, do you know where they tore down this house?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah. Well, that was my dad's first brother -in -law. His wife passed away in 696. And all them come together. They all worked the radio.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, they all took up the land here at the same time?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They all took up land here together. What we said in here. And then, Olsons did come in. Even though wasn't certain where we talked a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl, did he ever tell you what time it was?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I think he said it was the late 80s. Late 80s, close to 1890, maybe 88 around in there. And all they come in later then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what's this about?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He worked out on the railroad when he was first homesteading him. He had built a cabin and he would leave it. He built a cabin so he'd have a home. You see, you had to build on it. You had to make an improvement. And you had to spend so many months, three months or a year, you had to spend on the land to file your, to put your claim in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, when would he leave to go work on the railroad?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, that was being explained to you when the broke up said he could go to work again. See, that was all done, not like grading, all done with horses. And they had to have it when it was taut grounded, not too wet. Then they went back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just left the place here?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He just went, when they left in the spring of year, they worked the plot around the cabin. Get all grass in that way, safe or fired. Come home to fall while the Indians had all but war.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: So, you can see they kept, took better care of their land than the white men are doing. There was no brush, all you found, just to be yellow vines, no little timber in between.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Now, Dad, Dad had trees out there. It was better than four foot under stump, but he saw down and burnt for the midfield. And he could stand on top of the hill over there in the lion's face. He was either back in the field or he could look right through the tree. He came across the whole farm.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's better than half a mile.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Just open pine country.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And then there was some sheep men come in. They come in here too. Later, I think, the West Indies come in here with the sheep. They're not a quick bottom down here on the ford did joining. There used to be a big sheep crowd. And there used to be sheep crowd up on Alton's place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, who do you worry?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They come up from Washington with the sheep. Yeah, there were some guys around the big bank, especially that were grazers. They just came through here and used all of it as they wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any trouble between them and the sheep eating up the land?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, you see, there was no cultivation. It was just a passion. It was a free open range all over. They could go clean up the cell cribber. It was the only place they wanted to see.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's what they did. They went back in there and they all backed the helmer. You see, you come there and there was that shop based there east of Helmer. Well, you go right down straight there to publish. And there used to be sheep crowd. Thank you. There was a sheep crowd. That I remember. That's far back where we used to go fishing. You could still see that there was sheep crowd there in an old cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father, did he actually have to walk out all the way from here to Plumb?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, there was no horses. No transportation, no railroad, no nothing. Then he said to walk. Then that bunch went together like Dent, Halston, Sambignon. They all figured when we were ready to go to work, but they all hit up for Plumber.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And when I go to the streets, I just walk out after Moscow best started. I just had to take a pack and I went back and pack it home. He packed more than one sack of flowers from Moscow. It must have taken him a day or two just to get in there and back walking. No, it took him a couple of days. A good day to go in and back.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They said they had no idea how to put the packs. They had a sack of flowers on their back and had some coffee and stuff, but they needed to pack out.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Then after a few, I forget what your bus is, and he was working for Toner if there was a mosque and then he'd buy me a little saddle horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then he had a saddle horse too, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there was no Troy then?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No mosque at first. There was nothing. Louis didn't. I seen a picture of Louis then. That's just him. Square front like the old building room. That's the picture of Louis then. That's where they were on that picture there, showed where they filed on their land office. That's where they all had to go to file on their plans, was to Louis then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when he could stop having to go out and work out and could just stay here and work here? Do you know how, was it a long time that he had to work out?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, he worked out for quite a few years while he was clearing land. Not like in the wintertime when he was home, then he'd fall to timber for clearing land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that timber get to the sawmills or was it before anyone else?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Burnt it. Burnt all them big boys. No sawmills.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I forget what year that Bowman mill started, or Peyou Hansen, he started first meal. Right there were bandals, meat markets, meatches on the brick bottom. That's where they all their first logs.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And then Bowman, he started to solve the right, where he took out that big cut down there with that house, he says he was not from bandals, fixed up there. On that new road, well on the left if you look, still shows kind of where the mill fallen was. That was where the Bowman started at. That was Bowman's meal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these others come in about the same time too?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: The other mills around here, what about O .K.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Olsen?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Oh, that come way later. That was way later. That come later than that. That did. It was up to 1900 when O .K. got in, I guess. It was a laughter in 1900.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about right around here, where were the mills that were in this area around Dry Creek?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, Troy Lumber Company, they had one mill right there in here where you made the turn. That was standard mill. Then they had the white pine mill and the old white pine mill. And then O .K. Bowman had one mill on Big Bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a mill called the Dry Creek or the Dry Ridge Mill?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Dry Creek. That was up there right back of the gun tub. Up there were the drop pallets, rocks, and the Dry Creek mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take them a long time to clear up the land?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Real slow. Yeah, they didn't have much feeder for quite a while. You see, they had to get worked up so they have enough so they could afford to feed the team of horses. That was the first.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And there wasn't much, very big horses. 1 ,100 pounder coyuses to break up the land.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I remember the first team they had, that was 22, 24 years old, and I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it like for you when you were a kid growing up out here?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Better than... better if we had more fun and better times than we did.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You could talk to any of them like Carlos and them now. They'll tell you that the kids have the enjoyment we did. We'd all get together and always had rich parties, women parties, school -house dancers. So we didn't have like to have a strange way of taking the car and taking off.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: We had... I'll say it, we had all the time, those kids. And one thing, you don't have what we had then, and that is good hunting and fishing right now. All I'd have to do go down here in the past year and then holler and come back with 18, 20 fish. Nice talk. And if we went down to the back, we'd go down to the home mine up here in Long Island, down there in the top right there, come back home with over 150. No limit to the road.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: So you could see it out there. And blue grass by the hundreds. Great big bunches up in the fields. Like the mountain grass, they'd up like that. You'd chase them up to 6 -8 hundred.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I remember after Dad got there, first 22, we had a lot of birds. Then after each... my brother brought a 16 -year -old shotgun, and after that, we had to get the bird with the all through the summer.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Now you've got to hunt for anything. You can't talk blue grass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a lot of the food that you ate then, wild food from wild animals? A lot of the regular food in your diet?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Oh yeah. Well, yes. I'll tell you, that blue grass, that's good meat. Something like this Chinese person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have too long?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, but I didn't find it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the way you did it?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, I know you've been there last week, never really destroyed. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Probably just... And then they always had a stand, set up the stand beside over here. Had the choir singing, had the piano, all took the church organ there. Had the administrator's sermons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a custom from Sweden to have at Midsommar?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Oh, Sweden. They never... they wouldn't think about working on Midsommar day.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You couldn't have been much more than nine or ten in that picture. Yeah, well, there was a tool there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the school like when you went to school? What was it like for you to go in that little schoolhouse?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, all I guess, just about, what, the same as you go to the rates, I want to think you had one teacher for all the rates. From the first to the eighth. But, probably we learned just as much, you know, more in the public than most schools, and you do now, ladies. You take my eighth grade, you get arithmetic. There's a picture of the school up there.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: See, we had algebra on the last of our eighth grade. You don't find that now. No, we really had a good there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's old school, huh?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's sitting up there by Kenneth's house now, Kenneth Hawkins. He had it right there, and there were his grain bins, and he pulled it up there. Useful shop.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That wasn't to amend, or I remember when we had 32 shoveling gourds at school one year. At schoolhouse was crowded.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Alan Gordon was our teacher at that time.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That teacher there, that's Christian Ryerson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you start working around the house when you were pretty young, when you were working on the place?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I was 14, I started calling it a foot boat.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You know what a foot boat is, just a walking pond, three horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right after you quit school?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No, before. You see, yeah, we all had school certain times a year.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: We had this first grain brick that gave the help.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, I called it my name when I had walked this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That short, you see. You're short, so yeah. I got it sitting down there beside the old house, the old plow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah? Yeah? By then, was your father just making a living doing farming by that time?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Farming and cattle.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's what he did.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Look, in those days, we were watching that a lot of cows were so clean. Now I hear eight to twelve cows to milk.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Oh, I see here.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I think you can see a few of those who put a lot of cattle. This one here was about 24 feet. I've been there since, without just the nighting, but back over. That had room for about that many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid, what did it look like around here? Was it largely cleared off by then, or did it still have mostly timber around the place?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: When you were young, I think you could see now all that light and finish yesterday and then when they were the timbers, I broke up a strip over there. Once you take in, all you take in, that's the field over there.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That back end, I broke that. Then you take over here east,

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: 35, 30 acres of that, I broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the walking plow, huh?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Three horses. Well, the last, probably 15 acres, then I had a cat and a big breaking plow. That's the biggest part, but then with three horses. Now I hear. I tell you about that first kid. Yeah, that's them. Here's your if and the addition here.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's his first king, or his old, when this picture was taken. This one's cold. That's from that part of the addition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He and that same team for, what, 20 years or better?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: The addition here, I think he was 20, was both old friends, in addition to the same two years older.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: It's really something to me that they could work so many years on that kind of tough work. Well, you use them on the wagon. Use them all the way in and out. Put them down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where he'd take his grain to try?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I'm going to put the sacks exactly.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That was rough on horses across their rocks. The tongs had to pull over rocks and get a rock in their tongs, rip for them back into the horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how often do you think you would go into trying in the Euro days when you had the wagon and team?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: One choice, really. Now, like when they were selling cream, Sam would be taking one day, Martin would take one day, Dad would take it one day. Well, that was a very good idea. He just did that. He wanted to eat anyway. Always wanted to eat at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to go into trying to get rid of that?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, I did that. I was just going to go out there. I went to town, I was going to eat at the town.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And those days, that old boy came in and said, I'm going to get a kid to come in at the time of my address. We'll find a big paper sack and fill her up with candy. Or you'll come home with a great big paper sack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the storekeepers do that for you and give you the candy?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: All kids, and you can come in and fill your hands with more Dutty. When you're going to fill them up, you can take that candy. Sounds like a pretty good deal. You can take your cake, put it in for ten cents. You've got more candy, you buy for four bits now.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And then cookies, they had that in 30 to 50 gallon drums, wooden barrels. You can take that if you want, anyway down to your package.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You know, a grocery sack, that big, that high, that would be too big for the gin. You'd have to order some gin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, just two packages, would you buy them?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have kids you had to be friends with and try that you'd play with when you were in town?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, there's actually a few. Like the Peterson kids. They used to come out and go over here to church. You had folks who didn't, and they used to come over here to church. They'd come here for dinner. So I had their Peterson's and different kids there for I knew. Those two days you got to quit with people, you shouldn't do no things. And they wanted a mix. The friendly people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Troy was a pretty tough town in those days, back in the early days?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Troy really was not a tough town. Never. Okay, I think you find nowadays, like Marsford, there's a tougher town today, and Troy was one of the kids. Never seen much trouble around town.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Okay, people talk more of getting together, indoor station, what they do nowadays.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I like you have a dance, a teleporter in Troy, or to the Bowery out in Randall's flat, or everybody's there, peaceful, never seen much.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Good music. And then up there, as you come in from Marsford, up there, from Bubba Ross, just a little bit of that house was up there. They used to be around the hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Used to be what?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Around the hall. Oh. There they had Dan King and then Roger Skating.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: It sounds like there was a lot of different places to go for entertainment, a lot more than nowadays. Yes, and then you had Community Hall and Big Bridge, and then you had the big around Holland pavilion for Dan King Skating and Riting Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you go from one place to another and not feel like an outsider?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You couldn't get the same button if you'd always got to get the same button.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Up around Princeton and Harvard, people told me there used to be a fair bit of fighting at dances, and it'd be the group from Harvard to go down to potlatch or something like that, and they might mix it up. But it doesn't sound like you're saying something different around here. They didn't do that. Well, we used to. Out at Big Bridge, always had a nice time out there. Guess you left at the helm, well, kids fights. Get it out to something, but it wasn't much fighting up at Helmer. And out there at the bowers, buyers should flower in on the ground and fly. That's what all was useful, because then buyers just, they really kept the order. It was a big bandit. And you'd be all the way back to kind of the top of the ground here and you'd get the order.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much time for this stuff?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I mean, would you just be in the weekends and you'd be able to go around and... Yeah. I went right there. We could go to Big Bridge. We go Friday night and Saturday night we could go out there to the fireplace. And then there'd be schoolhouse dances in between.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Like after school, like around Christmas and New Year, in order to get school clothes, where they always had school parties.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how old were you during about this time you were talking about teenagers? Teenagers?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Teenagers, yeah.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Like you see in that picture here on... It used to be a get -together on Sunday things when I was growing up about 10, 12 years old.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: We had our church... Swans for the little toy here. Yeah. Well then as you go out to the highway you just go past Eddy's, where he's got the fence, and then make a little turn that's where he's right there. He's like, oh dear. And that is now the Lions Club in for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was that the church for everybody around Dry Ridge and also Bear Ridge?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Bear Ridge. In over a quarter of a century.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, it was quite a Sunday school. That church, if you will, put it well for them on Sunday. They had five and six classes in that church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like Sunday school pretty well?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, it was great. It was a custom to go to Sunday school. Folks always went to church here with Shandir, with kids to Sunday school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did you start working in the woods?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I said... 18th.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you start?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Just when the World War started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you start in at?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Up at Palmer. Up at Camp 6. First.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And then from there on went to different camps. So I did 14. I went to Camp 5. And I went to Camp 8 in Elk River.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I worked early in the Sunday years. And after these two, to fall, working to fall, but then I went to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the camps like? What were the living like in the camps?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, that's when the IWWs come in and Shandir started cleaning camps. We used to have these railroad cars, some of us on marion tracks. It was clean. You had your bat. Nice combination. Good kitchen, nice clean kitchen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when you started, or was it when you first started it was like that?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, that was more or less when you got up into the Goldrick and the other camps of North. That was different. You had to carry your bedroll. But then after IWWs got in, well then you got rid of the bedroll.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about the IWWs, about their coming in and stuff like that?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: You told me that you and a lot of other guys joined up because it was a good bunch. Well, it was that they wanted to get rid of the bedrolls and get clean accommodations in the camp. That's what the way it was for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did they organize?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, something like the CIO and the AFL is today. They had a man come around organizing the men. He signed up for a reunion.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's the way it was when I joined up with them down at the talks of camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When a guy came in and what did he do? Did he explain to you what they wanted?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They asked for clean bedroom rooms, no bedrolls, wanted sheets in the beds. Of course, we didn't get the clean blankets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about all this stuff that they spread against the IWW? Did you hear that before the IWW came in?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No, that was just propaganda against IWW. I was just trying to break it up because they wanted to farm a CEO and they were working class because they were not working class themselves. And then politicians talked against it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about oldie dolman that gave a speech against the IWW? Would you tell me about that one?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, that was the figure that the IWW was putting matches into green shocks like the Genesee and burning trash machines. But there was no IWW had any attention about that. I never heard any of the IWW speak about that. What was it was that they were red smut, which set lots of fires even after IWW went out of the talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it that the oldie dolman gave a speech in town? Was it saying that they were no good or that they were against the country?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They were doing this and that was against the farmer and what did they do in setting the fires. Because he knew, he was a banker and he knew that the IWW was trying to organize the pharmacy and with the labor party. Which was the biggest mistake the farmer's ever done when they didn't join up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they didn't join up with the IWW?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that's so?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, if it had been that, he'd have more to say for himself. It would be one thing, you wouldn't have to go to town and just be asked what you give me for this and what you want for that. They had more to say.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: It'd been better for the market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they wanted the IWWs, wanted the farmers to join?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, they did.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Because I was worried about what the farmer would come around and spoke to us, what he said about it. I even brought that to my brother and dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was for that?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That would be for their better. It would have been a big union. It would have been the labor and the farmer. It would have been a strong union, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that your father believed what only Bowman said?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, he did, because he didn't know what it was. What there was doing. He knew that there were cash machines for burning, but he didn't know the truth of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that you told me about how you came home?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was telling me how bad they were?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, he had been to town on Saturday, and that's the day that Bowman made that speech during the front of the bank. Then I came home from camp on Saturday evening, and he'd tell me about this. He kept talking about the bad diversion. I disagreed with him on this, and that's what I didn't think that the working classes had better people entering. Then also that you couldn't trust IWWs, but they might prank you around your home. Then after that, you had to read his speech, and I had to talk to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I threw my card in front of him on the table and asked him, What do you think?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No, it's just IWW here.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There used to be an old bridge down here across the road, because the old road just went right straight up across there. There was their names, card and names, IWWs, all over the bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the guys that were in it?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Those were the men working in Troy, walking home or driving home from camp on Saturday evenings, stopped there and card their name on that. Carving, they showed him IWW. Oh, that's a big hell of carving.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think, you say that most of the guys that worked in the woods around here got in it?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: They did. There were quite a few from Troy. There were IWWs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did your father think then? Did you persuade him?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I think, well, sure. I persuaded him that there was nothing like it. Because you might say they were not strangers in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they kept it pretty quiet then, if they did, or do you think they didn't care who knew about that they were for the Union?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I mean, back in those days. It seemed like a lot of people were so much against the IWW, at least that's what you hear now. That's what you hear. But, let's say it's one thing. Now my dad is telling about how dirty the camps were, the regular camps. Lawlessy entered in. He had to carry his own blanket rolls. Didn't have no bats or anything in the camps. That's one thing with IWW, you got out.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Now when Margaret Creek was running, that he had them all locked the buildings and you had to carry your blankets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do about, what did the guys do about bedbugs and stuff like that in the camps? Was there anything they didn't?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There was powders to use that had used that. But still you couldn't get rid of them. Them old log buildings, there was cracks and logs were full of them.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And not only that, but there were greybacks in camps. They had to watch out for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were greybacks?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's really human laws. That's what they were. And dad said in the regular camps, the camps were full of them.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: It's a sad day, you should call it grey sad. I don't know what's in it. Smear that on your body, then it's dry more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that your dad believed in quicksilver?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He had a little bottle, but that big. But that's great, just a little bottle. And they could sell her a net. And that's what he was told to carry on them when they crossed the ocean because them boats that had come on, they were a day. And so he carried that, and then when my brother come here, he had him had carried too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't work, you think?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's what they had claimed. That worked. Dad carried that in his bedroll and his bed in the logging in the regular camps. He carried that bottle with him. And dad said that he never had any trouble keeping free.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work for a long time in the camps? Me?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah. Well, off and on in the springs and falls. I did that until I left to have flown. Probably five years. And then me and my brother, we went to the farm from dad and went to farming together. First day and two when we got through the first few years. I'd gotten a cap in the fall of the year. It worked so well as not to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your farming operation like when you first started? What did you use?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Horse power. It was horses. In 1928, we let them buy their tractor. Horse tractor.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Of course, we had the horses then, too. We always kept five, six, had the horses as we had our tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you use them for just riding after that, or would you use them for the farm work?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: When I did with big horses, I had one team there who weighed 1 ,600 a piece. My brother had a team that weighed over 1 ,700 a piece. They were all big draft horses. First, I'm a member of them horses. We used them all. I'd do the plowing with the tractor and heavy like the disk in the spring. Then my brother, he'd use them for mass cultivation and seeding with the horses. He'd follow up behind me with the horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any guys in the camps when you were working that were really strong men?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Let's say the loggers. I know there are some places I hear about guys that were big drinkers. They were heavy workers, real big workers. They could lift up a log all by themselves. They could do. There were some pretty husky men. The strongest man that I know of was your wellest Negro. He lived there yesterday. He said the dairy helmet. He always had him roll down skinny with horses and rolled into decks on their landing. He'd break peavey handle after peavey handle. That man was strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wasn't too big a man, did he?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I thought he was pretty good at it. There was lots of big lumber yaks. I don't know how you hear that. There was lots of them up there. Over 200 pounders. We were working back in the one one. It was for Charlie Lacey. Then there was one steep side hill there that they couldn't orbit horses. He wanted someone to roll them down. That was a big fellow by the name of Bill Greenwood. He was there. He took the yogurt and he asked me if I'd run away to him and we'd log that side. He'd hand log that with a peavey.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's about 19 years old then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did the heavy work, did he?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He both had to work on a log. He rolled again and was jumped to get turned and kept going. That man had power and was strength in old Bill.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: What about there were some old timers around here, I know. Some of these guys that Carl has mentioned to me. I wonder what you remembered about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guys like Big Anderson, you knew him?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yeah, he was an old sailor. Retired down there. His legs were bad. He had very coarse veins. He must have turned cancer because he had big swords on his legs. That was a big man.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There's quite a few big ones around. You take Mr. Sandberg, Gus Sandberg up here. That was a big man. A strong man. Young Carl Olsen up here, big Carl they call him, that was a strong man.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There's quite a few up north. They're up in Pleasant Hill area. Some pretty husky men up in there. Like old man Swamburg and Charlie Swamburg. That was strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this about Big Anderson's...you said he had some kind of invention?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, he was working on one of what he said. Oh, the machine is supposed to run forever on its own. He had all kinds of gadgets on that thing.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I really couldn't explain just...yes, I had it built, but I see it. It was wrong rig, you know. It made something like a barrel type. And all kinds of propellers and different things on that. It's supposed to be a perpetual motion. It's supposed to be a perpetual motion. But he never got it to go until he passed away.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, I'd heard that he was a pretty frightening looking guy for some of the kids. He looked almost like a monster. Some kids with a big beard and his canes. He was. He was a monster. And he had done bad rig since he had two long canes when he walked to town. He walked to Troy once in a while. He had two long sticks there. Oh, he was better than six feet. I figured six -three.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And he was well -bearded, broad -shouldered, big man. Not fat, but just big -boned man.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: His legs, they never healed up. And they smelt as if bad odor was always had them wrapped. And when you take that off, where you see the person that was bandaged.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: We was down there and visited him. See, August Anderson, they lived just across, very quick from him. Wasn't too far to go. We'll go down there and some distance when we go and visit him. My dad always, August always went over there and us kids would go with him with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he used to talk about his sailing days at all?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Not too much. He didn't say too much. The only thing he's telling about that when he was a sailor, he always used to smuggle turkey cigars around at different places. And, you know, turkey, he'd come to Arab country and raise camels. And then they were going to arrest him there for smuggling cigars. And they had a camel there that was loaded. They'd go and eat it, grab that and put it in front legs and tip the old bag. And after that, scared them, they left him alone. The only thing, that's the only thing he ever told about his strength. I tipped that camel over. But he was a real strong man. Oh, he was a strong you bet. No doubt that that man was strong even in his old age. He'd make his own wood down there around the trees. No. Take a younger wood. See, 20 inches through, four footer, and he just take that up and tore it down his shoulders and knocked his cabin with it.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: So he had strength. He could walk without them canes when he was working. He could. But when he went off to walk in distance, he always could, to rest himself on his canes. His legs were bad.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There were a couple other guys that Carl said, and I think you mentioned one of them. I can't remember. There was one guy out by Deery that you mentioned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I'm thinking of?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He was an old character out there. There was one guy, Wild Bill, but that's not the one that I'm thinking of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Wild Bill?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: There's this other guy, Buffalo something or something. Cougar Jack. Cougar Jack, that's the one. Jack Strimmin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jack who?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Jack Strimmin. That was a big, strong man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why Cougar Jack?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Well, it was one cold, bad winter, about four feet of snow, and he was walking home out to Texas Ridge. And he met a cougar, and the cougar faced him in the fence right next to him. He broke the fence post off there and hit the cougar across the back and broke his back. He got the name Cougar Jack.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Cougar wasn't going to get away from him.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Nice, nice friendly man, Cougar West. I knew him when I started working in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the moonshining that went on around here? Was there a lot of it?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Yes, there was a lot of it. And they're all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any guys that were real big operators that made a lot of it?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Sam McKinn, who was pretty good, he was a big operator.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sam McKinn?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He had a big setup up in White Pine country.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And there was a shell. You'd take lots of them up in North Deer, Cleashing Hill, and Spring Valley. Lots of them up in Deer. There was lots of them that weren't trying to go along. Lots up around Erie. Hell, or Goldfellow too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of difference in the stuff? Some of it good and some of it not good?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Different. Different how they kept the coils from that thing. Used to be some off the rock and little bridge. Pretty hard to hold my cumber right back in the maintenance. He done quite well. Seasonal Emmet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seasonal Emmet?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Did guys...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have to be pretty careful not to get caught?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Very careful.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Sam McKinn, he offered me for many years and he never got caught. He was careful.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: That's one law that always seemed so crazy in me. I could never understand how they ever got that pass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what's the point of having a law that nobody's going to listen to?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Obviously there was probation at that time. And for a long time they could go into Washington and get it, and then Washington went dry, and then they'd go to Montana or ship the ducks from Montana. And after Montana went dry, I'd then get it out of Canada. There was always a place to get it. By the same time there was lots of moonshine going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know about any guys getting caught?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Quite a few. Quite a few that are caught.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I hear like, did you know Pat Malone at all? That guy from Louisville?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Sure did. Oh, Pat.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He's sitting there sharing the hotel all up there in the lobby and asleep.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: And the only thing is, it's Pat, he liked his whiskey as well as anybody else. He'd never made no rest. I'll find some of it at a bottle or...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just asked, have you got a drink?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: But if you handed Pat a bottle or the pint of whiskey, you might be sure he'd take your drink first or whatever you would get in here.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: I've seen that happen more than once in a four -year life celebration, except to... He'd see somebody with a problem, he'd sneak up behind him. Or Pat would get knocked and go to his room and lay down and sleep at home.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: He sounds like quite a guy to have for sure. Well, but you take the monster shirts, they had heavy deputy up there. And Pat was one that he kept good old people listened and they liked him. And he'd go along well with the people, so whenever there was a change in shirts, Pat always got the hub.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whenever there was what?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Changing the shirts offices. New shirts coming in, they'd always select. Didn't make any difference with the Democrats or Republican Party coming in, but Pat Malone always got that job in both of them.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: The idea I get is that people really liked him. He was liked, yes, he was liked. Not only in Boulder, but surrounding community. Pat was liked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear any other stories about him, like going after people or anything like that? Or stories about Pat?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: No. No. There seem to be a lot of stories about him around. Well, there could be lots of people that know quite a bit about him. But as far as me, I didn't know. I knew Pat and I was working at a camp up there to oversee Pat around and talk to him. From the camps you'd see him when I come in to Boulder. In the case of Pat, he'd always talk and talk to you if you knew he'd always talk and talk.

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: Pat, I'm sorry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you hadn't been farming for very many years yourself when that depression came along, had you?

speaker: Elmer Flodin
words: See, I sure found me a 20, 21, 22. When I started farming a little, we bought the stock and not the dot, bought that, bought the farm and then...
